meaning: A system of communication where both parties can send and receive signals simultaneously
example2: Modern network switches operate in full-duplex mode.
example3: Full-duplex radio allows pilots to talk and listen at the same time.
Explanation: 'Full-duplex' is a technical adjective from telecommunications and networking. It describes a communication channel that allows data to flow in both directions at the same time, like a telephone call. This contrasts with 'half-duplex' (walkie-talkies, where only one can talk at a time) and 'simplex' (one-way, like TV broadcast). It is a key feature for efficient, natural conversation and data transfer. The term is specific to engineering and computing but underpins everyday technologies like phones and modern internet connections.
usageTip: Use 'full-duplex' only in technical contexts to describe systems where two-way simultaneous communication is possible.
commonMistake: Using it in non-technical conversation. In everyday terms, you'd say 'like a phone call'.

example: The network protocol supports full-duplex communication.
example2: Full-duplex is faster than half-duplex for data transfer.
Explanation: The term 'full-duplex' refers to a communication system where data can be transmitted and received at the same time. This is standard in modern networking, whereas 'half-duplex' only allows one-way traffic at any given moment. It is a crucial concept for understanding network performance and throughput. Use this term when describing how your system handles concurrent data streams. It is an technical adjective describing hardware or protocol capability.
commonMistake: Confusing it with half-duplex where traffic takes turns.
